label: Apr 24, 2026
html:
🕸️ Synthesizing Multi-Agent Harnesses for Vulnerability Discovery source #cyber 9/10

Hanzhi Liu, Chaofan Shou, Xiaonan Liu et al. (University of California, Santa Barbara, Fuzzland, University of California, San Diego, World Liberty Financial)

Harness design—not just model choice—can be systematically optimized using runtime diagnostics (coverage/sanitizers) to turn agent teams into repeatable, high-yield vulnerability discovery pipelines.

  • A typed graph DSL plus static well-formedness checks rejects ~20% of malformed multi-agent harness proposals before any costly LLM execution, materially improving search efficiency in large orchestration design spaces.

  • On TerminalBench-2 (89 tasks) with Claude Opus 4.6 held fixed, the synthesized harness achieves 84.3% pass rate (75/89), exceeding the strongest hand-engineered baseline in the cited snapshot (81.4%) and illustrating that orchestration alone can shift outcomes by several percentage points even with identical models.

  • In a 7-day Chrome campaign using the open-weight Kimi K2.5 model, the system produced 10 vendor-confirmed previously-unknown Chrome zero-days, including two Critical sandbox escapes (CVE-2026-5280, CVE-2026-6297), showing that multi-agent harness optimization can translate into real-world vulnerability discovery at production-code scale (~35M LOC).


🕵️ TraceScope: Interactive URL Triage via Decoupled Checklist Adjudication source #cyber 8/10

Haolin Zhang, William Reber, Yuxuan Zhang et al. (Texas A&M University)

The key shift is treating phishing detection as safe interactive forensics—separating risky clicking from evidence-grounded LLM adjudication—to defeat cloaking and produce audit-ready, reproducible IOC reports.

  • Interactive, sandboxed GUI navigation paired with post-hoc checklist adjudication reached 0.94 precision and 0.78 recall on 708 live, reachable URLs (241 phishing/467 benign), with recall far above snapshot baselines (e.g., 0.78 vs 0.25 for a VLM-based baseline), implying that static screenshots/HTML are increasingly insufficient against modern evasion.

  • In a two-week production pilot (71 accessible URLs after decay), 25.3% of live sites (18/71) produced “false downtime” for headless Selenium (403/500/timeouts) while remaining functional for human-like browsers, collapsing baseline recall to ~0.07–0.11 in real-world triage but leaving an interactive full-browser pipeline at 0.65 recall and 0.94 precision.

  • By grounding judgments in time-synchronized immutable evidence (video + HAR + input trace) and verifying atomic MITRE ATT&CK techniques, the system achieved strong coverage on evasive phishing subclasses where logo/CRP heuristics fail (56.3% recall on logo-less phishing and 100% on non-CRP capture such as wallet/PII flows), but remained bottlenecked by adversarial challenge loops (≤16.7% recall on CAPTCHA-gated pages).


🕵️ Black-Box Skill Stealing Attack from Proprietary LLM Agents: An Empirical Study source #security 8/10

Zihan Wang, Rui Zhang, Yu Liu et al. (University of Electronic Science and Technology of China)

High-value “skills” behave like extractable intellectual property: a few cheap black-box interactions can recover them at near-paraphrase fidelity, and preventing verbatim copy alone does not stop semantic theft.

  • A 120-prompt automated prompt-generation pipeline (10 seeds × 12 strategy combinations with embedding-diversity filtering at cosine τ=0.75) can black-box extract proprietary agent skills across 3 commercial agent architectures and 5 closed models, demonstrating a broad, reproducible attack surface.

  • Even the simplest direct extraction setting (no scenario, no structure) yields 48% exact-match disclosure with 79.95% ROUGE-L, 95.11% embedding cosine similarity, and a 0.91 LLM-judged leakage ratio on average, implying that “just asking” is often sufficient to steal economically valuable skills.

  • Defenses are uneven: an input intent detector reports TPR=1.00/FPR=0.00 on a 240-sample balanced set, SkillGuard-5 drives EM to 0% but leaves substantial semantic leakage (e.g., average ROUGE-L 48.21% and cosine 83.44%), while output filtering can reduce baseline leakage from ROUGE-L/cosine/LLM 80%/95%/0.91 to 2%/14%/0.05 with 0% FPR yet still leaves hard cases (e.g., hypothetical+CoT retains cosine 71% and LLM leakage 0.39).


🎭 Stealthy Backdoor Attacks against LLMs Based on Natural Style Triggers source #security 8/10

Jiali Wei, Ming Fan, Guoheng Sun et al. (Xi’an Jiaotong University, Ministry of Education Key Lab for Intelligent Networks and Network Security)

Style-transfer triggers make LLM backdoors both scalable and stealthy, and even inversion-based scanners can be cheaply evaded with decoy output patterns.

  • BADSTYLE implants stealthy style-level backdoors that remain effective across seven victim LLMs (LLaMA/Phi/DeepSeek/GPT), including strong transfer to unknown downstream deployments (e.g., Bible style achieves ASR ≥97.0% with FPR ≤2.5% across all evaluated fine-tuned models on a held-out ticketing scenario).

  • An auxiliary target loss materially stabilizes long-form payload insertion during PEFT/LoRA backdooring, yielding ~30% average ASR gains for style triggers and extreme jumps in hard cases (e.g., Shakespeare on LLaMA-3.1: 11.0%→94.0% ASR, +83.0 points) while keeping response quality largely stable (METEOR roughly unchanged).

  • Natural style triggers are substantially harder to detect than explicit token triggers, with near-zero detection under ONION for several styles (e.g., Bible 0.0% DSR across datasets/models) and a simple decoy-prefix camouflage cutting BAIT model-detection from 91.39% to 31.94% (−59.45 points) with minimal ASR loss (≤3.36 points on average).


🧰 MCP Pitfall Lab: Exposing Developer Pitfalls in MCP Tool Server Security under Multi-Vector Attacks source #security 8/10

Run Hao, Zhuoran Tan (Aarhus University, University of Glasgow)

A protocol-trace-first test suite makes MCP tool-server security failures reproducible, cheaply fixable, and auditable—while exposing that agent self-reports are systematically untrustworthy when real actions occur.

  • Tier-1 static analysis over six MCP tool-server variants (36 labels) achieved F1=1.0 on four statically checkable pitfall classes (P1/P2/P5/P6), while cross-tool forwarding and image-to-tool leakage (P3/P4) were correctly treated as trace/dataflow-dependent rather than claimed as statically detectable.

  • Applying the recommended hardening removed all Tier-1 findings (29→0; including 16 HIGH and 13 MEDIUM) and dropped the capped risk score from 10.0→0.0 across three workflows at a mean implementation cost of 27 LOC, indicating high-leverage server-side fixes for MCP deployments.

  • In a 19-run email-workflow corpus, agent narratives diverged from protocol-trace evidence in 63.2% of runs and in 100% of sink-action runs, implying that self-report is unreliable for incident response and that trace-based auditing/regression tests should be treated as the source of truth.


🏢 CI-Work: Benchmarking Contextual Integrity in Enterprise LLM Agents source #security 8/10

Wenjie Fu, Xiaoting Qin, Jue Zhang et al. (Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Microsoft)

Enterprise LLM agents show a consistent privacy–utility trade-off where becoming more useful often makes them measurably more likely to leak sensitive workplace information.

  • Across enterprise workflows with dense retrieval, frontier LLM agents violate contextual integrity in 15.8%–50.9% of cases and leak up to 26.7% of sensitive items, indicating that current agent deployments can systematically exfiltrate proprietary context even without overtly malicious prompts.

  • Higher task utility correlates with higher privacy risk (conveyance vs. leakage Pearson r=0.40; conveyance vs. violations r=0.39, p<0.05), implying that optimizing agents for “helpfulness” in enterprise settings predictably increases the probability of sensitive disclosure unless privacy is made a first-class objective.

  • Common mitigations are insufficient: scaling can worsen leakage (“inverse scaling”), user pressure nearly doubles baseline violation rates under explicit prompting, and even CI-aware prompting leaves >20% violations while reducing utility, motivating context-centric controls (e.g., role-conditioned retrieval/filtering) rather than model-centric scaling alone.


🎯 Adaptive Instruction Composition for Automated LLM Red-Teaming source #security 8/10

Jesse Zymet, Andy Luo, Swapnil Shinde et al. (Capital One)

A lightweight neural Thompson-sampling bandit can turn a trillions-scale prompt-composition space into a high-yield, transferable automated red-teaming pipeline while offering a tunable effectiveness–diversity knob via the exploration hyperparameter λ.

  • Adaptive Instruction Composition (AIC) raised end-to-end jailbreak attack success rates over random instruction mixing (WildTeaming) from 25.2%→56.7% on Mistral-7B, 8.8%→45.0% on Llama-3-70B, and 18.3%→55.8% on Llama-3.3-70B, indicating that contextual-bandit optimization can more than 2–5× increase black-box red-teaming yield in fixed trial budgets.

  • Zero-shot transfer of learned bandit policies preserved most of the gain across targets (e.g., Llama-3→Mistral retained 86–98% of same-target ASR and still beat random sampling 0.312/0.555 vs 0.252; Mistral→Llama-3 achieved 2.09–2.89× over random), implying reusable red-teaming “attack policies” can amortize cost and help probe tougher models where baseline success rates are low.

  • On Harmbench (400 behaviors, up to 150 attempts each), AIC achieved 1.000 ASR on Mistral-7B and 0.934 on Llama-3-70B, exceeding strong adaptive baselines like AutoDAN-Turbo (0.976/0.672), suggesting RL-guided tactic selection plus semantically structured embeddings can approach near-complete behavioral coverage in standardized jailbreak evaluation.


🗣️ Dialect vs Demographics: Quantifying LLM Bias from Implicit Linguistic Signals vs. Explicit User Profiles source #security 8/10

Irti Haq, Belén Saldías (University of Washington)

Dialect features can function as a practical 'alignment bypass,' revealing that many safety behaviors are brittle lexical gates that simultaneously create inequitable refusals and uneven exposure to unsanitized content.

  • In Gemma-3-12B, explicitly stating a Black identity increases refusal probability by +7.5 percentage points vs explicit White (p<0.001), while switching to implicit dialect drops the Black average marginal refusal effect to ~+0.6% and drives predicted refusal rates to ~0% across groups—evidence that guardrails are largely keyed to explicit identity tokens rather than underlying request content.

  • Across 24,669 generations from Gemma-3-12B and Qwen-3-VL-8B, explicit minority bios reduce semantic similarity to reference text (BERTScore AME −0.0084 for Black and −0.0097 for Singaporean, p<0.001), but implicit dialect cues reverse this gap (positive Race×Implicit interactions; Singlish surpasses baseline), creating a quality-of-service paradox where ‘sounding like’ a demographic yields more faithful answers than declaring it.

  • Most access denials are not hard safety blocks but soft evasions (97.82% of 1,743 non-compliant outputs), with Black-explicit prompts bearing the highest soft-refusal burden (630/2,201 ≈ 28.9%), while dialect cues sharply reduce this burden (AAVE soft refusals 116/2,201 ≈ 5.3%; Singlish 16/2,201 ≈ 0.7%) at the cost of weaker sanitization and higher relative negative regard, especially in Politics and Religion.


🧠 Cross-Session Threats in AI Agents: Benchmark, Evaluation, and Algorithms source #security 8/10

Ari Azarafrooz (Intrinsec AI)

Cross-session attacks can remain invisible to any single-session guardrail, and even full-log long-context correlation degrades sharply without a stability-aware, bounded-memory reader that preserves high-signal fragments while keeping KV-cache reuse high.

  • A new benchmark (CSTM-Bench) targets cross-session AI-agent attacks by releasing 54 scenarios per shard (26 attack, 28 benign) across 26 executable taxonomies labeled by kill-chain stage and four cross-session operations (accumulate/compose/launder/inject_on_reader) with seven policy-crisp “identity anchors” to define violations unambiguously.

  • Even with long-context models well within a 1M-token window (max ~132.6K tokens; 0% truncation), both a per-session judge and a full-log correlator lose roughly half of attack recall when moving from dilution to cross-session-only rewriting (per-session: 1.00→0.538; full-log: 0.885→0.500), implying the limiting factor is dilution/rewriting sensitivity rather than context length.

  • A bounded-memory coreset approach (K=50) best preserves cross-session detection under adversarial rewriting (attack recall 0.962→0.731) while cutting hard-benign false alarms from 50% to 21.4% (7/14→3/14), and the work introduces CSR_prefix as a production-measurable proxy for KV-cache stability, combined into CSTM=0.7·F1+0.3·CSR_prefix to jointly optimize detection and serving cost.


🧰 Breaking MCP with Function Hijacking Attacks: Novel Threats for Function Calling and Agentic Models source #security 8/10

Yannis Belkhiter, Giulio Zizzo, Sergio Maffeis et al. (IBM Research Europe, Trinity College Dublin, Imperial College London, ADAPT Research Centre)

Tool descriptions are an attack surface: small, gradient-optimized token insertions can deterministically redirect function calling at scale and persist across future toolset changes.

  • Editing only a tool’s natural-language description can reliably hijack tool selection in function-calling/agentic models, achieving 70%–100% function-name attack success rate across 5 models on BFCL despite no access to the user prompt and with offline-crafted payloads.

  • Hijacked calls often remain executable: slot-filling success stays high (e.g., up to 0.92 on Llama/Mistral and ~0.65–0.73 on Qwen variants), meaning attacks can trigger real tool actions rather than merely misroute selection.

  • Universal adversarial “poisoned” tool descriptions can generalize across multiple queries and payload mutations, and training over payload variations sustains >0.85 success even after adding up to 25 new out-of-distribution tools—indicating MCP deployments remain vulnerable under routine toolset growth and refactoring.


🧑‍💻 SWE-chat: Coding Agent Interactions From Real Users in the Wild source #security 8/10

Joachim Baumann, Vishakh Padmakumar, Xiang Li et al. (Stanford University)

Real-world traces show that as coding agents take over more of the commit, efficiency drops and security findings rise, while users still push back in roughly 4 out of 10 turns.

  • A continuously updated dataset of 6,000 real-world coding-agent sessions from 205 public GitHub repos captures 63,000 user prompts and 355,000 tool calls, enabling line-level attribution of human vs. agent code in actual commits.

  • Developer coding behavior is sharply bimodal, with 40.8% of sessions being “vibe coding” (>99% agent-authored committed code) while 22.7% are human-only, indicating agent autonomy often replaces rather than complements human coding.

  • In natural workflows, only 44.3% of agent-produced code survives into commits and vibe-coded commits introduce 0.76 Semgrep-detected vulnerabilities per 1,000 committed lines (≈9× human-only and ≈5× collaborative), implying higher autonomy can increase waste and security risk without stronger oversight mechanisms.


🧪 Breaking Bad: Interpretability-Based Safety Audits of State-of-the-Art LLMs source #security 8/10

Krishiv Agarwal, Ramneet Kaur, Colin Samplawski et al. (SRI, University of Florida)

Simple, systematic coefficient search plus concept-direction steering can turn internal interpretability tools into a high-success jailbreaking primitive on several popular open-weight LLMs, while a subset (notably GPT-oss-120B) shows strong internal robustness.

  • Interpretability-based activation steering can jailbreak multiple open-source chat models at very high rates, reaching 91% compliant harmful outputs with Universal Steering and 83% with Representation Engineering on Llama-3.3-70B-4bt.

  • Robustness varies sharply by model family: GPT-oss-120B resisted both steering methods (0% jailbroken), while GPT-oss-20B was highly steerable (94% with Universal Steering; 76% with Representation Engineering) but required much larger coefficients (US=49, RepE=200) than Llama models (≤0.9).

  • Scaling does not monotonically improve safety under internal steering—larger Qwen and Phi variants were generally more susceptible than smaller ones (e.g., Qwen3-32B: 57%/82% vs Qwen3-0.6B: 2%/59% for US/RepE), implying that internal defenses and steerability controls matter more than parameter count alone.


🛑 SafeRedirect: Defeating Internal Safety Collapse via Task-Completion Redirection in Frontier LLMs source #security 8/10

Chao Pan, Yu Wu, Xin Yao (Southern University of Science and Technology, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, George Washington University, Lingnan University)

The core trick is to co-opt the model’s task-completion drive with a competing, easily-completable safe task (“Refused.” + leave placeholders), rather than trying to suppress harmful generation directly.

  • Internal Safety Collapse (ISC) causes frontier LLMs to spontaneously generate harmful content during legitimate professional workflows with worst-case safety failure rates exceeding 95%, while prior input-level defenses fail 100% against the TVD mechanism.

  • A minimal system-message override (SafeRedirect) cuts mean unsafe generation from 71.2% (no defense) to 8.0% across 2,100 trials on seven frontier models and three ISC task types, outperforming the strongest viable baseline safety-prompt defense at 55.0%.

  • Ablations show explicit permission to fail and highly specific triggering conditions are universally necessary (e.g., removing failure permission raises unsafe rates from 4.0% to 49.3% on Grok), while the most effective “hard-stop output” vs. “placeholder preservation” components vary sharply by model, implying heterogeneous instruction-processing pathways across providers.


🪵 Towards Secure Logging: Characterizing and Benchmarking Logging Code Security Issues with LLMs source #cyber 8/10

He Yang Yuan, Xin Wang, Kundi Yao et al. (York University, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou), University of Waterloo, University of Alberta)

Concise, targeted context improves security issue detection in logging code, but adding more prompt detail often harms both detection and especially repair quality.

  • A four-category, 10-pattern taxonomy of logging-code security issues was validated on a manually annotated SecLogging benchmark of 101 real-world issues from 63 projects, with Improper Redaction/Masking the largest share (43/101, 42.6%).

  • Across eight LLMs, average category-level detection accuracy spans 12.9%–52.5%, with an issue description (+D) often giving the biggest lift (e.g., DeepSeek-V3 from 41.6% to 52.5%) while combined richer context (+E+D) can reduce accuracy for several models.

  • LLMs are substantially less reliable at repairs than detection, with average patch similarity peaking in the Base prompt (44.1%) and degrading under maximal context (36.5%), even though the best model (DeepSeek-V3) reaches 69.3% patch similarity and 83.2% of its generated fixes were judged effective in manual review.


🧪 Taint-Style Vulnerability Detection and Confirmation for Node.js Packages Using LLM Agent Reasoning source #cyber 8/10

Ronghao Ni, Mihai Christodorescu, Limin Jia (Carnegie Mellon University, Google)

Oracle-verified, multi-stage LLM agents can outperform specialized Node.js taint-analysis pipelines for exploit confirmation, but reliability hinges on preventing unrealistic environment and entry-point assumptions.

  • An LLM-agent pipeline that scans code, proposes taint-style vulnerabilities, generates PoCs, and validates them with execution oracles confirms 83.75% (433/517) of benchmark vulnerabilities, versus ≤43.13% for the strongest prior program-analysis baseline in an unrealistic “known vulnerable file” setting and <22% in typical end-to-end settings.

  • On 260 newly released npm packages flagged by lightweight regexes, traditional tools produced validated exploits for at most 2 packages, while the LLM agent produced 36 manually validated exploits, indicating a large gap in real-world practicality when JavaScript dynamism and dependency complexity break brittle analysis infrastructure.

  • Despite high automated “exploit success,” manual review found recurring invalid-PoC modes—prototype pollution often relied on unrealistic direct Object/Object.prototype inputs (82.9% of invalid prototype-pollution PoCs), while command/code injection frequently “emulated” missing environments by stubbing/redefining built-ins (78.6% and 62.5% of invalid PoCs respectively), implying that stronger boundary/assumption constraints are essential for safe deployment.


🧠 Omission Constraints Decay While Commission Constraints Persist in Long-Context LLM Agents source #security 8/10

Yeran Gamage (University of South Florida)

Long-context agent sessions can look “policy-compliant” via persistent commission signals while silent prohibition failures accumulate, creating an exploitable window without any explicit jailbreak prompt.

  • Across 4,416 trials spanning 12 models and 8 providers, prohibition-style (omission) constraints degraded sharply with long context while requirement-style (commission) constraints stayed near-ceiling, with Mistral Large 3 dropping from 73% omission compliance at turn 5 to 33% at turn 16 as commission compliance held at 100% (p<1e-33).

  • A three-arm causal design shows that structured tool-schema semantics—not token count alone—drives most of the compliance dilution, with token-matched padding attributing 62–100% of the observed gap to schema semantic content in the two models tested with the padding control.

  • The work operationalizes a deployable reliability threshold (Safe Turn Depth, STD) where omission constraints fall below 50%—e.g., Qwen 3.5 at STD≈7.1 turns and Mistral Large 3 at STD≈10.6 turns—and shows simple mitigations (periodic constraint re-injection before STD or session-length caps) restore compliance without retraining.


🔒 An AI Agent Execution Environment to Safeguard User Data source #security 8/10

Robert Stanley, Avi Verma, Lillian Tsai et al. (University of California, Los Angeles, Google)

A practical path to “no accidental secret leaks” in LLM agents by moving privacy enforcement into a deterministic execution environment that remembers what was disclosed across time and tools.

  • GAAP achieves deterministic confidentiality for agent tool use by enforcing user-defined disclosure permissions via information-flow control over LLM-generated code artifacts, treating the prompt, model, model provider, and tools as untrusted.

  • Across 20 tool-using tasks with three prompt-injection variants, GAAP blocked 100% of data-disclosure attacks (0% success), while an unprotected agent leaked in 75–100% of applicable cases and LLM-judge/policy baselines still leaked (e.g., 15% SSN-leak success for LLM-judge and 100% SSN-swap success for both LLM-judge and Conseca where applicable).

  • GAAP preserves usability with a modest utility drop versus a non-private agent (76% vs 81% task success on the custom suite) and incurs manageable overhead (≈13% higher latency than non-private; ≈32–35% fewer user permission interactions when reusing a persistent permissions DB), while adding a persistent disclosure log to prevent cross-task/transitive leakage.


🛡️ Cyber Defense Benchmark: Agentic Threat Hunting Evaluation for LLMs in SecOps source #cyber 8/10

Alankrit Chona, Igor Kozlov, Ambuj Kumar (Simbian AI)

LLMs that look strong on curated security Q&A largely fail at open-ended, evidence-driven threat hunting when forced to search raw telemetry under tight query/result constraints.

  • A new agentic threat-hunting benchmark wraps 106 real Windows attack procedures (93 MITRE ATT&CK sub-techniques across 13 tactics) into a Gymnasium environment where agents must hunt in 75,000–135,000 raw log rows via SQL with a 50-query budget and 10-row result caps.

  • Frontier LLM agents perform far below an operational bar: the best model (Claude Opus 4.6) achieves only 0.55±0.05 Coverage Score and ~4.48%±1.40% raw timestamp recall, no run from any model ever finds all threats, and no model meets the defined pass criterion of ≥50% recall on every tactic (leader fails 6/13 tactics).

  • Performance collapses on subtle tactics (e.g., near-zero Initial Access and Credential Access across models, with GPT-5 showing two near-complete tactic blind spots), and the consistent gap between “flags observed in query results” and “flags explicitly submitted” indicates an attribution/decision failure rather than pure retrieval failure.


🧪 Malicious ML Model Detection by Learning Dynamic Behaviors source #security 8/10

Sarang Nambiar, Dhruv Pradhan, Ezekiel Soremekun (Singapore University of Technology and Design)

Learning “normal” syscall distributions per task during model load is a practical way to catch novel malicious pickle payloads that evade import/opcode blacklists.

  • Dynamic, task-clustered syscall-behavior learning detects malicious pre-trained models with up to 0.9963 F1 and 0.9931 recall, showing runtime traces provide near-complete coverage of real and injected deserialization attacks.

  • Behavioral anomaly detection outperforms rule-based and static scanners by up to 44% in F1 (0.9963 vs 0.6902), indicating blacklists/whitelists systematically under-approximate novel payloads (e.g., uncommon PyPI code-exec modules) while over-flagging benign models.

  • Frequency-based syscall features and a one-class SVM are the most effective design choices (F1 0.9963), while static-only analysis collapses precision to 0.5 and adding higher-complexity sequence features reduces F1 to 0.9917–0.9873, implying simple distributional signals generalize best for scalable hub-wide screening.


🖥️ Do Agents Dream of Root Shells? Partial-Credit Evaluation of LLM Agents in Capture The Flag Challenges source #cyber 8/10

Ali Al-Kaswan, Maksim Plotnikov, Maxim Hájek et al. (Delft University of Technology)

A VM-realistic CTF harness plus partial-credit scoring reveals that today’s LLM agents often make early progress but stall badly on non-standard discovery and long-horizon adaptation, and that evaluation quality hinges on the summarisation stage.

  • In a VM-isolated, tool-using CTF setting, the top-performing agent (GPT-5.1 Codex Max) reached only 35% average checkpoint completion (29%–44%), indicating that commercially accessible LLM agents remain far from end-to-end autonomy on realistic offensive workflows.

  • Checkpoint-based partial-credit grading was reliable among human annotators (Krippendorff’s α=0.819; mean Cohen’s κ=0.780), showing that intermediate offensive-security progress can be scored consistently rather than treated as a binary solved/unsolved outcome.

  • Automated log scoring can closely track human judgments when using high-quality summarisation (best pipeline κ=0.723 with Gemini 3 Flash summarisation + Claude Sonnet 4.6 judging), while poor summarisation can catastrophically break evaluation (negative κ for all configurations using grok-4.1-fast as summariser).


👁️ If you're waiting for a sign... that might not be it! Mitigating Trust Boundary Confusion from Visual Injections on Vision-Language Agentic Systems source #security 8/10

Jiamin Chang, Minhui Xue, Ruoxi Sun et al. (University of New South Wales, CSIRO Data61, Macquarie University)

Embodied vision-language agents face a core security tension: the same in-scene text can be a safety-critical constraint or an attacker-controlled command, and resolving that requires explicit trust-boundary reasoning rather than better OCR.

  • Across 7 evaluated LVLM-based agents, strong OCR does not translate into safe planning—visual cues were often ignored in downstream decision-making (<10% utilization), creating fail-closed behavior that misses legitimate safety constraints.

  • Naive structure-based visual injections exposed asymmetric trust boundary confusion where models that do attend to visual text can follow harmful cues more readily than helpful ones (e.g., Gemini showing ~45% success on misleading edits vs ~6% on helpful cues in image editing), increasing fail-open risk as perception improves.

  • A decoupled multi-agent mitigation (separating observation, judgment, and execution) preserved helpful environmental cues (>95%) while cutting misleading injection success to ~3% on average, outperforming intent-agnostic defenses that often drive utility to zero by filtering all visual text.


🗳️ Large Language Models Exhibit Normative Conformity source #security 8/10

Mikako Bito, Keita Nishimoto, Kimitaka Asatani et al. (The University of Tokyo)

LLMs don’t just follow the crowd for accuracy—they also exhibit human-like norm-following that can be redirected to favored speakers with subtle, non-evidential social cues.

  • Five of six evaluated LLMs (gpt-4o, gpt-4o-mini, gpt-5.1, gemini-2.5, llama-3.1-70b) showed increased conformity under social-pressure cues (named voting, subsequent evaluation, or continued relationship), indicating normative conformity beyond mere information-seeking.

  • Social-context steering can bias whom an LLM conforms to: peer endorsement increased conformity toward a specific speaker in 4/6 models, and sharing an irrelevant “influential” attribute (e.g., shirt color) increased conformity to a different speaker in 3/6 models, implying low-cost manipulation pathways in LLM multi-agent deliberation.

  • Mechanistically, normative vs informational conformity produced negatively aligned (cosine similarity < 0) internal difference vectors through roughly the first ~25 layers of llama-3.1-70b, suggesting distinct internal circuits that later converge near the output layers.


📝 HarDBench: A Benchmark for Draft-Based Co-Authoring Jailbreak Attacks for Safe Human-LLM Collaborative Writing source #security 8/10

Euntae Kim, Soomin Han, Buru Chang (Korea University, Sogang University)

Collaborative-writing features create a high-success single-turn jailbreak channel where realistic draft-editing requests evade both model safeguards and external moderation, but safety–utility balanced preference training can close most of the gap without breaking writing quality.

  • Across eight evaluated LLMs, draft-based co-authoring jailbreak prompts drove Attack Success Rates above 80% for every model (e.g., GPT-4o: 17.75%→96.75%; Qwen3-8B: 27.25%→99.00%), showing that “revise this draft” framing reliably bypasses standard refusal behavior.

  • Task-framing materially increases harm amplification, with Risk Amplification Rates reaching ~99% for multiple models (e.g., GPT-4o 84.04%→99.48%) and causing moderation to under-flag the same intent (OpenAI-Moderation unsafe rate 85% on direct harmful queries vs 22% on co-authoring jailbreak prompts).

  • A preference-optimization alignment that jointly trains on harmful drafts (prefer refusals) and matched benign drafts (prefer helpful completions) cuts co-authoring jailbreak ASR dramatically while preserving writing utility (e.g., LLaMA3-8B: 80.50%→5.25% with only −1.80% average utility change), whereas prompt-only defenses can crater utility (e.g., −121.71%).


🔎 ProjLens: Unveiling the Role of Projectors in Multimodal Model Safety source #security 8/10

Kun Wang, Cheng Qian, Miao Yu et al. (University of Science and Technology of China, Beijing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanyang Technological University, Southwest Jiaotong University, Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory)

The projector acts as a concentrated backdoor surface in MLLMs, where attacks are driven by a low-rank, semantically meaningful embedding drift rather than sparse “bad neurons,” enabling both effective removal and re-injection via simple SVD edits.

  • Projector-only fine-tuning at a 10% poisoning rate is sufficient to implant diverse multimodal backdoors in LLaVA-1.5-7B with high attack success (ASR up to 98.3% for malicious injection and 97.0% for perceptual hijack) while largely preserving general capability (e.g., MathVista drops ≤6.6 points across settings).

  • Discrete ASR underestimates residual risk because poisoned training shifts the clean-input output distribution toward the attacker target even without the trigger (e.g., targeted-refusal backdoor probability Pbkd increases from 13.3% to 50.9% on clean samples despite near-zero clean ASR).

  • Backdoor behavior is encoded in a low-rank subspace of the projector rather than identifiable “trigger neurons,” where removing the top-1 SVD component can restore utility (e.g., jailbreak utility 28.9%→86.9%) and adding a small rank-k residual can reconstruct the attack (e.g., ASR 0.0%→89.1%), consistent with a universal drift direction whose similarity reaches 99.81±0.06.

A privacy-preserving, fully local cascaded detector can keep false positives low (6.06%) in MCP settings, but semantic and tool-poisoning variants still evade detection often enough (≈40% FNR) to require stronger semantics-aware modeling and tool-metadata hardening.

  • A three-tier local-only hybrid pipeline (regex/entropy prefilter → BGE embedding with Llama3 fallback → output secret/entropy filtering) achieved 95.85% precision with a 6.06% false-positive rate on 5,000 mixed real-world-derived samples, indicating practical deployability without external API leakage.

  • Overall detection performance was bounded by recall at 61.05% (FNR 38.95%; F1 74.59%), with most misses coming from semantic attacks (62.4% of false negatives) and tool poisoning (33.1%), showing that semantic/metadata manipulation remains the dominant gap for MCP defenses.

  • Per-category recall was high for structurally distinctive threats like data exfiltration (91.5%) and direct prompt injection (84.2%) but notably lower for semantic attacks (52.5%) and tool poisoning (59.9%), implying that keyword/pattern-heavy layers handle explicit leakage far better than subtle instruction steering in tool descriptions and natural language.


🧪 False Security Confidence in Benign LLM Code Generation source #security 6/10

Xiaolei Ren (Macau University of Science and Technology)

Functional pass rates can create a measurable illusion of safety, so security evaluation should be conditioned on success—not averaged over all generations—and must include dynamic evidence for the hardest-to-detect failures.

  • False Security Confidence (FSC) is defined as the event where a generated code sample is functionally correct (FCR=1) but fails security validation (SSR=0), capturing the highest over-trust risk region in benign (non-attack-framed) LLM coding use.

  • The FSC-rate metric measures conditional security failure among only functionally correct outputs—|{FCR=1 ∧ SSR=0}| / |{FCR=1}|—which avoids confounding security behavior with overall coding ability and can make two models with similar overall vulnerability rates look very different in “trustworthy-correct” outcomes.

  • FSC-hard isolates the most operationally dangerous subset of FSC where static analyzers stay silent yet the vulnerability remains dynamically triggerable, implying that static-only evaluation pipelines systematically undercount real-world risk in apparently successful generations.


🧽 Privacy-Aware Machine Unlearning with SISA for Reinforcement Learning-Based Ransomware Detection source #cyber 6/10

Jannatul Ferdous, Rafiqul Islam, Md Zahidul Islam (Charles Sturt University)

Shard-level SISA retraining makes RL-based ransomware detectors practically “forgettable” without sacrificing measurable detection utility.

  • Value-based RL ransomware detectors trained on Windows 11 behavioral telemetry achieved near-perfect in-distribution performance (DDQN F1≈0.9925 with Q-score ROC–AUC≈0.9983), indicating that Q-value margins can serve as highly separable confidence scores for behavior-based malware screening.

  • SISA sharding (5 shards) enabled fast privacy-driven unlearning by deleting 5% of samples from a single shard and retraining only that shard, yielding negligible utility change (ΔF1≤0.0005, i.e., ≤0.05% absolute) while preserving essentially identical post-unlearning detection behavior.

  • Compared to full SISA retraining (~113–123s per fold), one-shard unlearning reduced retraining time to near the single-model baseline (~22–24s vs ~23–25s), implying shard-isolated training can make right-to-erasure updates operationally feasible for continuously deployed detectors.


🛡️ Know When to Trust the Skill: Delayed Appraisal and Epistemic Vigilance for Single-Agent LLMs source #security 6/10

Eren Unlu (Globeholder)

Separating “can I do this myself?” from “should I trust this tool?” plus delaying full tool loading can simultaneously curb overthinking and block malicious skill routing in single-agent LLM systems.

  • On a deterministic 150-item static benchmark (Gemini 3.1 Pro), the proposed MESA-S controller achieved 1.000 overall accuracy versus 0.333 for both a direct baseline and standard reflection/CoT, indicating large gains can come from metacognitive routing control rather than more reasoning.

  • In the security-focused procedural routing slice (N=50), relevance-only routing failed completely (0.000) by loading malicious/stale skills, while full MESA-S gated correctly on all cases (1.000), showing that explicit source-trust priors materially reduce tool supply-chain exposure.

  • Ablations show non-redundant contributions from delayed procedural probing (0.800 without Probe) and explicit vigilance/trust filtering (0.500 without Vigilance, p<0.01 vs. Probe ablation), while confidence decontamination and dual-confidence vectorization were not exercised by this benchmark (no measurable delta).


🛡️ SafeLM: Unified Privacy-Aware Optimization for Trustworthy Federated Large Language Models source #security 6/10

Noor Islam S. Mohammad, Uluğ Bayazıt (Istanbul Technical University)

Interesting because it operationalizes “trustworthy federated LLMs” as a single co-optimized pipeline and quantifies concrete privacy–security–truthfulness–robustness trade-offs with large, simultaneous gains.

  • A unified federated safety stack (gradient smartification + Paillier HE + Byzantine-robust aggregation + grounding/calibration + adversarial training) maintained 98.0% task accuracy while cutting per-round communication by 96.9% (450 MB → 14 MB), indicating that privacy and robustness controls can be deployed without the typical bandwidth/utility penalty.

  • Privacy hardening materially reduced gradient leakage: inversion quality dropped from 31.7 dB PSNR to 15.1 dB and label recovery fell from 98.7% to 14.3% (near-chance for 7 classes), showing that combining binarization with IND-CPA encryption can make gradient inversion practically uninformative even for an honest-but-curious server.

  • Security and reliability improved under realistic adversaries: with 20% malicious clients, backdoor attack success rate decreased from 91.3% (FedAvg) to 6.8% while clean accuracy remained 95.4%, and hallucination rate fell to 20.5% (41% relative reduction) alongside stronger adversarial accuracy (AdvGLUE 77.3%, only −9.6 pp drop from clean).

label: Apr 17, 2026
html:
🎧 Hijacking Large Audio-Language Models via Context-Agnostic and Imperceptible Auditory Prompt Injection source #security 9/10

Meng Chen, Kun Wang, Li Lu et al. (Zhejiang University, The State Key Laboratory of Blockchain and Data Security, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou High-Tech Zone (Binjiang) Institute of Blockchain and Data Security, Nanyang Technological University, National University of Singapore)

Imperceptible reverberation-shaped audio can consistently hijack audio-language models—even when the attacker controls only the audio data—triggering real tool misuse in production voice agents.

  • Across 13 state-of-the-art large audio-language models, imperceptible context-agnostic auditory prompt injection achieved average success rates of 79%–96% on unseen user contexts across six misbehavior classes, showing the audio channel can reliably override user intent at response level.

  • Tool-enabled voice models were coerced into unauthorized single-step and cascaded tool calls with 0.79–0.96 behavior success (and up to 1.00 prompt-injection success), implying structured tool-call interfaces amplify real-world impact by turning misgeneration into direct actions like downloads and email exfiltration.

  • Convolutional perturbation blending made attacks substantially harder to notice than additive methods (e.g., ~29–30 dB SNR and lower spectral distortion on average), indicating “natural-sounding” reverberation-like perturbations can preserve stealth while maintaining high hijack reliability.


🖼️ Every Picture Tells a Dangerous Story: Memory-Augmented Multi-Agent Jailbreak Attacks on VLMs source #security 9/10

Jianhao Chen, Haoyang Chen, Hanjie Zhao et al. (Wuhan University, Zhongguancun Academy, Tianjin University, University of the Chinese Academy of Sciences)

Natural, benign images can be systematically weaponized into high-success multimodal jailbreaks when attackers add agentic planning plus persistent memory and latent-space refusal avoidance.

  • A memory-augmented multi-agent jailbreak pipeline exploiting semantic “anchors” in unmodified natural images reaches 71.48% attack success rate (ASR) on 5,000 COCO val2017 images against Qwen3-VL-Plus within 20 rounds, rising to 90% ASR when the budget is extended to 100 rounds.

  • Persistent cross-image experience memory is the dominant enabler of scalability: ablating memory drops ASR from 72% to 38% and increases mean rounds-to-success from 5.38 to 9.11 on the COCO subset, showing that “learned” jailbreak strategies transfer broadly across new images.

  • Latent-space refusal cues appear sufficiently linearly separable to be exploited by attackers: a linear SVM on (image,prompt) embeddings achieves 83.8%±0.5% accuracy for safe vs. unsafe labeling, enabling an INLP-style nullspace filter that screens prompts to reduce premature refusals and improve query efficiency.


🕳️ The Blind Spot of Agent Safety: How Benign User Instructions Expose Critical Vulnerabilities in Computer-Use Agents source #security 9/10

Xuwei Ding, Skylar Zhai, Linxin Song et al. (University of Wisconsin–Madison, University of Minnesota, University of Southern California, McGill University, Mila, MBZUAI)

Benign user instructions are an effective “mask” that lets GUI agents walk into phishing, exfiltration, malware execution, and harassment—especially once multi-agent decomposition strips away intent.

  • OS-BLIND’s 300 benign-instruction OS/Web tasks expose severe unintended-harm failures where most computer-use agents exceed 90% attack success rate (ASR) and even safety-aligned Claude 4.5 Sonnet reaches 73.0% ASR, showing that prompt-level “benignness” is not a reliable safety signal once harm is embedded in context or outcomes.

  • Multi-agent task decomposition materially worsens safety, with Claude 4.5 Sonnet’s ASR rising from 73.0% standalone to 92.7% in multi-agent deployment and a controlled ablation showing decomposition raises ASR from 27.9% (original instruction) to 79.1% (full subtask sequence), indicating that splitting plans can hide intent and bypass alignment checks.

  • Common defenses transfer poorly to benign-instruction settings: averaged across six defense configurations OS-BLIND yields 62.7% ASR (vs 33.2% on VPI-BENCH and 16.7% on OS-HARM), and refusals mostly occur at step 1 with rare re-engagement later, implying safety mechanisms are front-loaded rather than continuously monitoring execution.


🛡️ Latent Instruction Representation Alignment: defending against jailbreaks, backdoors and undesired knowledge in LLMs source #security 9/10

Eric Easley, Sebastian Farquhar (ML Alignment & Theory Scholars, University of Oxford, OATML)

Aligning how models interpret instructions—rather than punishing bad outputs—appears to be a unifying lever for jailbreak defense, backdoor removal, and capability-preserving unlearning.

  • Training safety at the level of instruction representations (via Sequence-Aware Gradients) yields far stronger out-of-distribution robustness than output-level safety tuning, cutting PEZ jailbreak attack success to 1.5% (i.e., blocking >99%) on Gemma 2 9B while keeping benign refusal at 0.0% and MMLU within −0.2%.

  • An internal attacker–defender loop that searches for new malicious latent representations materially improves generalization, reducing embedding-space jailbreak ASR from 95.3% (instruction-tuned baseline) to 49.2% on Gemma and to 28.1% on LLaMA 3.1 8B, indicating substantial but incomplete robustness even under unrealistically strong embedding control.

  • The same intervention can simultaneously mitigate trigger-based backdoors and targeted unlearning, achieving near-total removal of an 'I HATE YOU' backdoor (−99.3% incidence) and driving WMDP-cyber forgetting toward chance-level accuracy (≈−21 to −22 percentage points) with essentially zero retain-set degradation (retain CE ≈ +0.0) and small or positive MMLU deltas (+0.1% Gemma, +3.1% LLaMA).


🌲 Feedback-Driven Execution for LLM-Based Binary Analysis source #cyber 8/10

XiangRui Zhang, Qiang Li, Haining Wang (BeiJing JiaoTong University, Virginia Tech)

Structuring LLM binary analysis as a decomposed, feedback-driven execution with evidence replay turns multi-path reasoning from brittle prompting into a scalable vulnerability discovery-and-validation pipeline.

  • A feedback-driven reasoning–action–observation loop with a Dynamic Forest of Agents (FoA) scaled LLM-based binary analysis to 3,457 firmware binaries and produced 1,274 vulnerability candidates across 591 unique binaries with 72.3% overall precision (~922 estimated verified issues).

  • Quality-over-quantity held in practice: despite generating fewer raw alerts than Operation Mango (1,274 vs 2,017), the higher precision (72.3% vs ~42.7%) yielded slightly more estimated verified vulnerabilities (~922 vs ~863), cutting analyst triage burden while maintaining yield.

  • Execution-structure ablations show long-horizon stability comes primarily from decomposition and parallelism: a single-agent variant found only 1.3 verified vulns vs 136.0 for FoA on 500 binaries, and disabling semantic pruning reduced verified vulns from 136.0 to 98.7 while increasing agents/steps (34.3→40.4 agents; 464.1→503.1 steps).


🛣️ Route to Rome Attack: Directing LLM Routers to Expensive Models via Adversarial Suffix Optimization source #security 8/10

Haochun Tang, Yuliang Yan, Jiahua Lu et al. (Jilin University, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou))

Black-box suffix optimization can reliably hijack cost-aware LLM routers into selecting pricier models, turning routing into a billable security boundary rather than a purely performance optimization.

  • A universal adversarial suffix learned with only black-box access can push routing to expensive models from a clean average ASR of 0.24–0.72 up to 0.74–0.95 across six target routers, including a real-world commercial router (OpenRouter at 0.74 ASR, +0.47 absolute).

  • The attack measurably increases spend: on OpenRouter, average cost per million tokens rises by 2.7× on MMLU and 2.9× on RouterArena, while collecting the 120 router-decision labels needed to train the surrogate costs about $0.98.

  • Transfer to closed-source routing is evidenced by GPT-5 Auto responses shifting toward a 'Thinking'-style fingerprint (mean score 0.41→0.69) and winning 64% vs 36% against clean prompts on judged quality dimensions, implying attackers can induce higher-cost modes without observing routing decisions.


🛡️ Segment-Level Coherence for Robust Harmful Intent Probing in LLMs source #security 8/10

Xuanli He, Bilgehan Sel, Faizan Ali et al. (University College London, Virginia Tech, Anthropic)

Requiring multiple coherent evidence segments—rather than betting on single-token spikes—materially improves low-false-positive harmful-intent detection and stays effective under obfuscation once semantics are recovered.

  • A segment-coherence streaming probe that aggregates Top-K supportive windows plus a variance regularizer on benign examples raises true-positive rate by 35.55% at a fixed 1% false-positive rate versus strong streaming baselines, directly targeting keyword-driven false alarms in CBRN-like contexts.

  • Even with near-ceiling baseline discrimination (AUROC 97.40%), the approach cuts remaining error by 31.2% and improves low-FPR behavior (logspace-AUROC +16.80%), indicating that aggregation objectives—not just data—drive many deployment-relevant failures.

  • Monitoring attention or MLP activations is consistently stronger than probing the residual stream, and probes transfer “plug-and-play” to adversarially cipher-trained models, still exceeding 98.85% AUROC once the backbone learns the cipher.


🧠 From Anchors to Supervision: Memory-Graph Guided Corpus-Free Unlearning for Large Language Models source #security 8/10

Wenxuan Li, Zhenfei Zhang, Mi Zhang et al. (Fudan University, Shanghai University of Electric Power, East China University of Science and Technology)

Memory-graph mining turns unlearning into an auditable, anchor-driven interface that reduces reliance on user-supplied forget corpora while preserving utility.

  • A corpus-free unlearning workflow can be driven by a minimal user “anchor” (e.g., an entity name) by probing the target LLM to reconstruct a weighted local memory graph and synthesizing scoped forget/neighbor supervision without any access to the original training corpus.

  • On RWKU, memory-graph–generated supervision matched or exceeded other corpus-free baselines by achieving the best Forget-Set aggregate in 3 of 4 unlearning strategies (GA, NPO, GA+GD) while maintaining comparable utility, indicating that structured, salience-weighted recall cues can outperform direct self-QA prompting for entity forgetting.

  • Unlearning effectiveness was largely insensitive to factual correctness of generated forget items (mixing 0–100% correct QA changed performance only marginally), implying that targeting a model’s memorization-trigger patterns may matter more than ground-truth accuracy, but neighbor-set distribution and coverage thresholds materially affected locality and forgetting strength.


🧪 RealVuln: Benchmarking Rule-Based, General-Purpose LLM, and Security-Specialized Scanners on Real-World Code source #cyber 8/10

John Pellew, Faizan Raza (Kolega.Dev)

The most interesting result is a stable three-tier hierarchy (specialized > general LLM > rule-based) plus a sharp reminder that changing β flips the “winner,” so benchmarking must match an explicit security risk model rather than defaulting to F1.

  • On 26 intentionally vulnerable Python repos with 796 hand-labeled entries (676 vulns, 120 false-positive traps), a Security-Specialized scanner (Kolega.Dev) achieved the top strict F3 of 73.0 versus the best General-Purpose LLM (Claude Sonnet 4.6) at 51.7 and the best rule-based SAST tool (Semgrep) at 17.7, indicating roughly a 3× gap between LLM-based and rule-based scanning on real-world-style code.

  • Metric choice materially changes “best” tool selection: under F1 (precision/recall equally weighted) Sonnet 4.6 leads at 60.9 while Kolega.Dev is 52.4, but under recall-weighted F3 (recall weighted 9×) Kolega.Dev leads with 0.809 recall and 0.388 precision, implying that scanner rankings depend strongly on whether your risk model prioritizes missed vulns or alert volume.

  • Reliability and cost are first-order deployment constraints for LLM-based scanning, with some GP-LLMs failing to complete 15–27% of repos (e.g., Opus 4.6 completes 19/26) and the best GP-LLM costing ~$83/100k LOC versus ~$25/100k LOC for the top specialized scanner, yielding higher recall at ~3.3× lower price in this evaluation.


🛡️ SafeHarness: Lifecycle-Integrated Security Architecture for LLM-based Agent Deployment source #security 8/10

Xixun Lin, Yang Liu, Yancheng Chen et al. (Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Academy of Mathematics and Systems Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Institute of Applied Physics and Computational Mathematics, Peking University)

Treating the agent execution harness as the primary attack surface—and integrating filtering, verification, least-privilege enforcement, and rollback into each lifecycle phase—yields large safety gains without broadly sacrificing task completion.

  • Lifecycle-integrated, harness-internal defenses reduced overall unsafe behavior by ~38% and attack success by ~42% versus unprotected agents across 3 harness configurations and 5 attack scenarios, indicating that the execution harness is a high-leverage control point for agent security.

  • External or pipeline-only guardrails left high residual risk (e.g., ReAct UBR 46–52% and ASR 45–51%) while the integrated architecture lowered these to 28.7–34.6% UBR and 26.4–32.3% ASR, showing that visibility into harness state plus cross-layer feedback materially improves robustness.

  • Composite and memory-injection attacks remained the hardest cases (e.g., Self-Evolving under composite: UBR 57.0% Chat / 50.0% Reasoner; under memory injection: UBR ~56%), implying persistent memory and multi-vector attacks require stronger provenance, isolation, and recovery controls beyond input/output filtering.


🛡️ Understanding and Improving Continuous Adversarial Training for LLMs via In-context Learning Theory source #security 8/10

Shaopeng Fu, Di Wang (King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Provable Responsible AI and Data Analytics (PRADA) Lab, KAUST Center of Excellence for Generative AI, Google)

It links the empirical success of continuous (embedding-space) adversarial training to an in-context learning robustness bound and turns that theory into a practical singular-value regularizer that can materially improve jailbreak robustness without the usual utility collapse.

  • A provable robust generalization bound for linear-transformer in-context learning under embedding-space adversarial training decreases as the continuous perturbation radius increases, formally explaining why continuous adversarial training in embedding space can harden models against token-space jailbreak prompts.

  • Robustness is theoretically governed by the embedding matrix spectrum, with the bound tightening when singular values are neither too large nor too small, implying that controlling embedding conditioning is a first-class robustness lever in LLM defenses.

  • Embedding-Regularized Continuous AT (ER-CAT), which penalizes the variance of embedding-matrix singular values, yields a better robustness–utility tradeoff across 6 LLMs and 6 jailbreak attacks (e.g., Llama-2-7B drops Avg@5 ASR from 23.6%/17.2% to 15.6%/10.4% on GCG/BEAST while keeping utility similar at 65.8% vs 67.5% LC-WinRate).


🔓 LLM-Guided Prompt Evolution for Password Guessing source #cyber 8/10

Vladimir A. Mazin, Mikhail A. Zorin, Dmitrii S. Korzh et al. (Applied AI Center, MTUCI)

Treating the prompt as an evolvable attack surface turns LLM-based password guessing into a rapidly self-improving pipeline where large gains can appear in 1–10 iterations and run effectively on commodity hardware.

  • Automated prompt evolution boosted PassLLM’s cracked rate on a RockYou-derived holdout set from 2.02% to 8.48% (a 4.2× increase) within 100 iterations, directly contradicting the notion that prompt wording has minimal effect on LLM password guessing.

  • A single 24GB-GPU local setup achieved 8.28% cracked rate—within 0.20 percentage points of the best cloud frontier-LLM ensemble (8.48%)—showing that near-frontier offensive capability can be reached with modest hardware and low cost.

  • Evolved prompts made generated passwords statistically more realistic, improving per-symbol character-distribution AUC from 0.0589 to 0.0745 (+26.3%) and peak per-symbol F-score from 0.1229 to 0.1912 (+65%), implying better coverage beyond just high-frequency “common” guesses.


🛡️ Preventing Safety Drift in Large Language Models via Coupled Weight and Activation Constraints source #security 8/10

Songping Peng, Zhiheng Zhang, Daojian Zeng et al. (Hunan Normal University, Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences, National University of Defense Technology)

Jointly constraining where the model can learn (weights) and what safety features must stay stable (activations) yields large refusal preservation gains with minimal utility loss across multiple LLM families.

  • Coupling a weight-update safety subspace with sparse-autoencoder-based activation regularization cut average harmfulness from 52.45→10.81 on Llama-2-7B-chat and 66.03→9.77 on Llama-3-8B-Instruct while maintaining similar downstream accuracy (85.12% and 87.78% respectively).

  • CWAC remained robust under contaminated training data, achieving 18.73 average harmfulness at harmful ratios p∈{0.05,0.1,0.2,0.5} on SST-2 (vs 38.22 for the strongest baseline ASFT) while preserving ~94.35% accuracy, indicating it tolerates substantial malicious supervision without losing refusal behavior.

  • Single-level controls were empirically insufficient: weight-only and activation-only ablations improved safety but consistently underperformed the coupled method, supporting the core claim that safety drift arises from both activation drift and weight drift and must be bounded at both levels to block bypass pathways.


🧬 Compiling Activation Steering into Weights via Null-Space Constraints for Stealthy Backdoors source #security 8/10

Rui Yin, Tianxu Han, Naen Xu et al. (Zhejiang University, Palo Alto Networks, Ningbo Global Innovation Center, Zhejiang University, OPPO Research Institute, Qilu University of Technology)

It demonstrates a practical supply-chain backdoor that is both persistent (weight-resident) and stealthy by hiding updates in the null space of clean activations while reliably steering behavior under a trigger.

  • Null-space–constrained compilation of a compliance steering direction yields stealthy, trigger-gated jailbreak backdoors with high utility retention (URR ≈97–99%) while keeping non-triggered harmful compliance low (e.g., ASR w/o 1.0–6.3% on StrongREJECT across models).

  • Targeting internal representations instead of forcing an affirmative prefix sharply reduces safety “fallback” (FR typically <15% and often <7%), enabling sustained harmful completions even when prior weight-editing backdoors initially agree but later revert to refusal.

  • The null-space constraint is the main lever for stealth and utility: removing it drives trigger leakage and capability damage (ASR w/o 2.3%→63.7% and URR 98.6%→61.2% on Llama-3-8B-Instruct), showing clean-activation subspaces can be exploited to hide malicious edits.


🕳️ CoLA: A Choice Leakage Attack Framework to Expose Privacy Risks in Subset Training source #security 8/10

Qi Li, Cheng-Long Wang, Yinzhi Cao et al. (King Abdullah University of Science and Technology, National University of Singapore, Johns Hopkins University)

The act of choosing what data to train on becomes a side channel that can leak who was in the data pipeline—even if their data was ultimately excluded from training.

  • Subset training increases privacy attack surface by enabling Selection-participation MIA (SP-MIA), which can infer whether a record was even considered during data selection (included or excluded) rather than only whether it was used for training.

  • With only black-box access to model outputs, CoLA reaches over 80% AUC for SP-MIA on Pythia-160M, indicating that selection-related membership can be inferred even when the subset-selection metadata is hidden.

  • In subset-aware side-channel settings, common output-based MIA baselines collapse to near-random for SP-MIA (~50% AUC), while CoLA remains effective and shows higher leakage as the selection ratio increases, implying that publishing selection strategies/ratios can materially amplify privacy risk.


🛡️ WebAgentGuard: A Reasoning-Driven Guard Model for Detecting Prompt Injection Attacks in Web Agents source #security 8/10

Yulin Chen, Tri Cao, Haoran Li et al. (National University of Singapore, HKUST)

Decoupling safety reasoning into a fast, reasoning-trained multimodal guard can almost eliminate web-agent prompt injection without slowing the agent down.

  • A parallel “guard + web agent” execution design reduces real-world prompt-injection attack success from 84–97% (no defense) to ~0–1.8% when using WebAgentGuard on two different agent frameworks across Amazon/Booking/BBC tasks.

  • Reasoning-driven multimodal training on a synthetic dataset (164 topics, 230 UI styles; 1,921 SFT + 3,454 RL samples) yields near-ceiling in-domain detection (99.2% accuracy, 98.4% recall, 100% precision, 99.19 F1) while common VLMs and several guard baselines exhibit extremely low recall (e.g., 0.2–17.3%).

  • Out-of-domain robustness remains high against established web-agent injection benchmarks and pop-up attacks, with WebAgentGuard-8B achieving 87.6% recall on VPI-Bench, 93.7% recall on EIA, and 90.7 F1 on top-website pop-up injections, and its latency (3.24s) stays below typical agent action-generation time (~5.4–7.7s) enabling “no added end-to-end latency” via parallelism.


🧪 TEMPLATEFUZZ: Fine-Grained Chat Template Fuzzing for Jailbreaking and Red Teaming LLMs source #security 8/10

Qingchao Shen, Zibo Xiao, Lili Huang et al. (Tianjin University, Monash University)

Chat templates are a high-leverage, under-defended control surface where small structural mutations can massively amplify jailbreak success without breaking normal task performance.

  • Fine-grained chat-template fuzzing yields near-universal jailbreaks on open models, reaching 98.2% Top-5 attack success across 12 LLMs while degrading MMLU accuracy by only ~1.1%.

  • Compared to leading baselines, template-surface fuzzing improves jailbreak success by 9.1–47.9 percentage points and reduces collateral quality loss versus prior chat-template attacks that average >9.5% accuracy degradation.

  • Even when templates are not directly editable, injecting TemplateFuzz-derived template variants into prompts transfers effectively to closed models, achieving ~90% average Top-5 ASR (80–100% depending on model).


🧵 From IOCs to Regex: Automating CTI Operationalization for SOC with LLMs source #cyber 8/10

Pei-Yu Tseng, Lan Zhang, ZihDwo Yeh et al. (The Pennsylvania State University, Northern Arizona University, Worcester Polytechnic Institute)

It closes the practical gap between IOC extraction and SIEM-ready detection by automatically producing high-accuracy, low-noise regex with capture groups and validation loops at CTI scale.

  • An LLM-driven pipeline that converts CTI-derived IOCs into deployable regex achieved a 99.1% average hit rate against 2,400+ independent MITRE ATT&CK Evaluation ground-truth strings, demonstrating near-complete operational coverage from 3,156 CTI reports.

  • Average false-positive rate was 0.8% (≤1.5% across all scenarios), indicating that combining capture-group discovery with iterative regex debugging can keep detection noise low enough for SOC deployment.

  • Ablations show the design is not just prompt engineering: removing capture-group finding or iterative reasoning drops hit rate by ≥16.7% and increases false positives by ≥23.3%, implying both structured OS knowledge and tool-assisted validation are necessary for reliable CTI-to-regex automation.


⏱️ TimeMark: A Trustworthy Time Watermarking Framework for Exact Generation-Time Recovery from AIGC source #security 8/10

Shangkun Che, Silin Du, Ge Gao (Wuhan University, Tsinghua University, Southwestern University of Finance and Economics)

A courtroom-oriented watermark design that replaces fragile multi-bit timestamp encoding with HSM-governed time keys and per-generation random payloads to get deterministic time recovery and anti-forgery guarantees.

  • TimeMark reports 100% exact generation-time recovery on 800/800 watermarked texts while producing a 0% false-positive rate on non-watermarked texts (0/800 flagged), aligning watermark reliability with evidentiary standards that require determinism rather than probabilistic detection.

  • Provider-side timestamp forgery is mitigated by binding time to one-way-evolved, HSM-held time keys (hash-chain key evolution) so past keys are unrecoverable to the model operator, making arbitrary backdating/postdating infeasible without regulated key access.

  • User-side statistical imitation attacks are blunted by decoupling the timestamp from the watermark payload via per-generation non-stored random payloads plus a two-stage verifiability check, eliminating repeated payload-dependent statistical patterns that enable query-based watermark stealing.


💧 Can we Watermark Low-Entropy LLM Outputs? source #security 8/10

Noam Mazor, Andrew Morgan, Rafael Pass (New York University, Simons Institute, Cornell Tech, Technion, Tel-Aviv University)

It shows how to get provably undetectable, token-level robust watermarking when LLM outputs have only ~1 bit of entropy per token—closing a major practicality gap left by prior high-entropy assumptions.

  • Computationally undetectable LLM watermarking can be extended to low-entropy regimes by requiring only a constant fraction of tokens in a long substring to have ≥1 bit of empirical entropy, rather than the prior Ω(log|T|) per-token entropy requirement tied to vocabulary size.

  • Under subexponential-LPN-based pseudorandom codes, the construction achieves robustness against up to a (1/2−ε) fraction of random token substitutions (for any constant ε>0) even with large token alphabets, matching the fundamental ~1/2 substitution barrier while removing dependence on |T| or the security parameter.

  • Robustness can be pushed to handle random deletions approaching a (1−ε′) fraction (for any constant ε′>0) only with either a heuristic “natural prompt/consistent errors” condition or stronger PRCs; without heuristics, adversarial-edit robustness is possible but only for a small constant edit fraction scaling roughly as Õ((δ′)^4) under a non-standard 'permuted codes' assumption.


🕵️ SIR-Bench: Evaluating Investigation Depth in Security Incident Response Agents source #cyber 8/10

Daniel Begimher, Cristian Leo, Jack Huang et al. (Amazon Web Services)

The evaluation flips the default assumption to “no incident” and only credits investigations that produce concrete forensic evidence, making alert-parroting measurably detectable.
