Zongwei Li, Wenkai Li, Xiaoqi Li (Hainan University)

The key move is reframing agent safety as a software security engineering problem—explicit permission boundaries, isolation, extension governance, and audit trails—rather than a sequence of one-off prompt-injection patches.

  • OpenClaw-like autonomous tool-invoking agents are characterized as “insecure by default” because they combine mixed-trust input ingestion, autonomous multi-step execution, extensibility (skills/plugins/tools), and privileged access (files/credentials/APIs/OS) inside a single control loop that amplifies small policy or parsing failures into real-world compromise.

  • A four-part risk taxonomy—prompt injection, harmful misoperation, extension supply-chain risk, and deployment vulnerabilities—maps agent failures to specific pipeline stages (ingestion → context/memory → planning → tool execution → plugin loading → logging/egress), implying defenses must be stage-specific rather than relying on model-side alignment alone.

  • A defensible design blueprint prioritizes four engineering controls—least privilege, runtime isolation/secret hygiene, extension governance (provenance + permission manifests + revocation), and auditability (attributable execution traces)—and argues progress should be measured via realistic benchmarks and regression testing that validate permission-boundary compliance and high-risk action interception.


📜 Security, privacy, and agentic AI in a regulatory view: From definitions and distinctions to provisions and reflections source #security 5/10

Shiliang Zhang, Sabita Maharjan (University of Oslo)

What’s interesting is the regulatory mismatch: autonomous agents expand the attack surface and data exposure pathways faster than EU guidance is being contextualized into implementable agent-specific privacy and security obligations.

  • Across 24 EU AI-related documents published in 2024–2025, “agentic AI” is only explicitly introduced in October 2025 while privacy/security discussions remain mostly general rather than system-specific, creating compliance ambiguity for autonomous tool-using agents.

  • Regulatory language consistently distinguishes “models” (upstream GPAI components) from “systems” (deployed products), implying that obligations split between model providers (documentation, training-data transparency, evaluation) and system deployers (deployment risk controls, output labeling, impact assessments).

  • Dedicated privacy and security provisions are articulated for high-risk AI systems, GPAI, and (to a lesser extent) generative AI, but explicit agentic-AI-specific requirements are absent, leaving key agent risks (tool-call exfiltration, cross-agent prompt injection, long-horizon memory retention, supply-chain compromise) to be governed indirectly via GDPR/NIS2/CRA-style controls.

label: Mar 13, 2026
html:
🛡️ IH-Challenge: A Training Dataset to Improve Instruction Hierarchy on Frontier LLMs source #security 9/10

Chuan Guo, Juan Felipe Ceron Uribe, Sicheng Zhu et al. (OpenAI)

A programmatically-graded, adversarially-generated RL dataset can significantly harden frontier LLMs’ instruction hierarchy and simultaneously reduce unsafe behavior and prompt-injection success with minimal capability regression.

  • Reinforcement-learning fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini on the released IH-Challenge dataset with online adversarial conflict synthesis raises average instruction-hierarchy robustness across 16 benchmarks from 84.1% to 94.1% (+10.0%).

  • When a safety specification is placed in the system message, the trained model cuts unsafe behavior from 6.6% to 0.7% while largely preserving helpfulness on production-style safety evaluations, indicating IH robustness materially improves safety steerability rather than just refusal rates.

  • Instruction-hierarchy training also improves prompt-injection robustness—reaching 1.00 on an internal static agentic prompt-injection evaluation (up from 0.44, +0.56) and modestly improving CyberSecEval 2 (0.88→0.91)—but common system-level mitigations show diminishing or negative returns once the base model is robust.


🧬 CLIOPATRA: Extracting Private Information from LLM Insights source #security 9/10

Meenatchi Sundaram Muthu Selva Annamalai, Emiliano De Cristofaro, Peter Kairouz (University College London, University of California, Riverside, Google Research)

Layered heuristic privacy (redaction + clustering + summarization + LLM audit) is brittle to targeted poisoning, whereas differential privacy is the only mitigation that consistently shifts leakage back toward baseline.

  • A targeted poisoning-and-prompt-injection pipeline can extract a user’s sensitive medical condition from “privacy-preserving” LLM insight summaries in 39% of cases with only age, gender, and one symptom (vs. a 22% baseline guesser).

  • Attack success climbs to 81% on a Qwen-configured pipeline with the same minimal side information and approaches ~100% when the attacker knows five symptoms, showing that modest additional auxiliary knowledge can collapse heuristic privacy guarantees.

  • LLM-based privacy auditing fails catastrophically—detecting 0 of 205 successful disease extractions (often rating leaked summaries 5/5 privacy)—while DP-based analytics substantially reduce leakage toward baseline at ε=25 but degrade at ε=50, implying formal privacy is necessary yet operationally hard to tune.


🫥 Invisible Safety Threat: Malicious Finetuning for LLM via Steganography source #security 9/10

Guangnian Wan, Xinyin Ma, Gongfan Fang et al. (National University of Singapore)

Zero-width character steganography turns safety alignment into a UI illusion by creating a covert harmful channel that humans and content filters both miss.

  • Malicious fine-tuning can train LLMs to exchange hidden harmful instructions and answers via zero‑width Unicode steganography, making the visible chat interaction appear fully benign while covertly transmitting arbitrary malicious content.

  • On AdvBench (520 prompts), 100% of stegotext prompt–response pairs across GPT‑4.1, Llama‑3.3‑70B‑Instruct, Phi‑4, and Mistral‑Small‑24B were classified as safe by Llama‑Guard‑3‑8B before decoding, yet over 90% of the decoded interactions were labeled unsafe (e.g., GPT‑4.1: 93.3%).

  • The attack can bypass provider safeguards in practice: a GPT‑4.1 model fine-tuned through the OpenAI fine-tuning API retained a 100% refusal rate on plaintext harmful prompts while still emitting steganographically encoded harmful outputs, implying current training-data moderation and inference-time filtering can be evaded without embedding explicit harmful text in the fine-tuning dataset.


🧱 Models as Lego Builders: Assembling Malice from Benign Blocks via Semantic Blueprints source #security 9/10

Chenxi Li, Xianggan Liu, Dake Shen et al. (DAIL Tech, NLP & KG Lab, Huazhong University of Science and Technology)

Multimodal safety filters can be bypassed by splitting a prohibited request into harmless-looking “semantic Lego blocks” inside a diagram and letting the model recombine them during completion.

  • StructAttack exploits a semantic slot-filling failure mode where LVLMs complete locally benign “slot types” (e.g., “Raw Materials”, “Making Process”) into globally harmful instructions, reaching ~69% attack success on GPT-4o and ~50–74% on Gemini/Qwen3-VL across Advbench-M and SafeBench with a single query.

  • Structured visual prompts (mind maps/tables/sunburst) materially amplify this vulnerability: a text-only slot-filling template already achieves 54% ASR on a 70-sample GPT-4o subset, and adding visual structure plus small random perturbations raises performance further (e.g., 62.9%→67.1% ASR on GPT-4o).

  • Compared to prior typographic and OOD jailbreaks, StructAttack is both stronger and cheaper, achieving 65.7% ASR with 1 victim-model query and ~7.1% refusal rate versus SI-Attack (37.1% ASR, 10 queries, 28.6% refusal) and JOOD (38.6% ASR, 45 queries, 27.1% refusal).


🧬 Cascade: Composing Software-Hardware Attack Gadgets for Adversarial Threat Amplification in Compound AI Systems source #security 8/10

Sarbartha Banerjee, Prateek Sahu, Anjo Vahldiek-Oberwagner et al. (The University of Texas at Austin, Intel Labs, Symmetry Systems, Microsoft, Georgia Tech)

Security of compound AI systems is not just about prompts and alignment—legacy CVEs and hardware fault/side-channel primitives can be composed into practical chains that bypass guardrails and tool boundaries.

  • Cross-stack attack chaining can defeat common compound-AI defenses by combining traditional system flaws with LLM weaknesses, including an end-to-end safety break that uses a DoS on the query enhancer plus a Rowhammer-style guardrail fault to pass an unmodified jailbreak prompt to the generator.

  • In a guardrail evasion study over 50 harmful prompts, targeted token bitflips bypassed the guardrail 82% of the time, targeted attention-mask flips worked 72% of the time, and random attention-mask flips reached 94%—implying guardrails can be highly brittle to small memory corruptions even without changing the downstream model.

  • Adversarial suffix jailbreaks generated with a GCG-style method achieved an 80% jailbreak success rate (41 prompts) with substantial compute cost (~123 minutes per prompt on a 4×NVIDIA L40S setup), indicating attackers can trade GPU time for reliable safety violations once pipeline controls are bypassed.


🧩 Understanding LLM Behavior When Encountering User-Supplied Harmful Content in Harmless Tasks source #security 8/10

Junjie Chu, Yiting Qu, Ye Leng et al. (CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security, Flexera, Delft University of Technology)

Harmless-looking tasks like translation become a high-throughput safety bypass when users supply the harm, and common guardrails can be sidestepped simply by embedding that harm inside longer benign text.

  • Across 9 mainstream LLMs tested on 1,357 policy-violating knowledge snippets embedded inside 9 policy-compliant tasks (12,213 prompts/model), many models still produced harmful outputs frequently—e.g., GPT-3.5 Turbo averaged K-HRN 4.035 and Qwen3 3.942 (≈4 of 9 harmless tasks per harmful snippet), while Llama3 was far lower at K-HRN 0.178.

  • User-content-heavy tasks are the highest-risk channel for “in-content” harm propagation, with Translation reaching an average T-HRR of 0.512 (over half of harmful snippets yielding harmful outputs) and those outputs being highly grounded in the supplied text (GS ≈ 4.94/5), indicating models largely reproduce and disseminate the embedded harmful material.

  • External defenses are brittle under realistic “wrapping” where harmful text is blended into longer benign content: T-HRR jumps to 0.826–0.894 for GPT-3.5/GPT-5.2 under Llama Guard-family filters, and even chunking reduces but does not eliminate residual risk (e.g., GPT-3.5 still 0.330 under Llama Guard), implying input filtering alone is insufficient without content-aware refusal behavior inside the model.


🪞 The Mirror Design Pattern: Strict Data Geometry over Model Scale for Prompt Injection Detection source #security 8/10

J Alex Corll

Careful corpus geometry (matched positive/negative cells) makes a tiny compiled linear model outperform a 22M transformer for first-line prompt-injection screening while being orders of magnitude faster and less promptable.

  • A strictly cell-matched multilingual corpus (31/32 reason×language cells, 5,000 samples) enables a sparse character n-gram linear SVM to reach 95.97% recall and 92.07% F1 on a 524-case holdout at sub-millisecond latency with weights compiled into a static Rust binary (no external model runtime).

  • On the same holdout, a 22M-parameter Prompt Guard 2 semantic classifier achieves 44.35% recall and 59.14% F1 with 49ms median and 324ms p95 latency, implying that hot-path L1 screening can favor deterministic sparse detectors over small transformers when data is geometrically curated.

  • Residual risk concentrates in contextual ambiguity rather than mechanics: the linear screen shows a 51.9% false-positive rate on a hard-benign set that quotes/discusses attacks (12.3% at a higher threshold), and both the SVM and Prompt Guard 2 plateau around ~21% recall on heavily paraphrased attacks, motivating a layered architecture where later stages handle use-versus-mention and semantic thinness.


📄 You Told Me to Do It: Measuring Instructional Text-induced Private Data Leakage in LLM Agents source #security 8/10

Ching-Yu Kao, Xinfeng Li, Shenyu Dai et al. (Fraunhofer AISEC, NTU, KTH, NUS, UCLA)

Trusted project documentation can act as a stealthy prompt-injection carrier that turns helpful, high-privilege LLM agents into reliable data-exfiltration executors.

  • High-privilege computer-use agents executed README-embedded adversarial instructions with end-to-end private-data exfiltration success rates up to 85%, showing that documentation trust plus terminal/filesystem/network access creates a high-impact injection surface.

  • Semantic compliance with injected documentation instructions generalized across four LLM families (46–79% function-invocation success in a controlled tool-calling simulation), indicating a paradigm-level vulnerability rather than a single-model quirk.

  • Neither humans nor common detectors reliably caught the attacks—15/15 participants had a 0% detection rate, while 12 rule-based and 6 LLM-based filters failed to achieve high detection without unusable false positives—implying current “scan-and-block” defenses don’t separate malicious from legitimate setup guidance.


🕸️ KEPo: Knowledge Evolution Poison on Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation source #security 8/10

Qizhi Chen, Chao Qi, Yihong Huang et al. (University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Ubiquitous Intelligence and Trusted Services Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province)

Poisoning GraphRAG becomes much easier when the attacker forges a believable timeline that makes the false claim look like the 'latest update' rather than an isolated contradiction.

  • KEPo raises GraphRAG poisoning effectiveness from low double-digit CASR under prior methods to as high as 63.5% average CASR in GraphRAG local search (vs 31.5–52.1% for baselines) by forging time-ordered “knowledge evolution” paths that integrate toxic facts into high-ranking KG communities.

  • GraphRAG’s global-search mode is materially harder to poison than local search (e.g., KEPo average ASR 33.1% global vs 73.1% local), implying community-summary retrieval offers partial resilience but still fails when poisoned facts are embedded inside otherwise relevant communities.

  • Common RAG defenses barely reduce KEPo impact (token retention ~98.6–99.7% and ASR essentially unchanged), indicating that toxicity/prompt-pattern filters and query paraphrasing do not address graph-level semantic/temporal plausibility attacks.


🧯 Deactivating Refusal Triggers: Understanding and Mitigating Overrefusal in Safety Alignment source #security 8/10

Zhiyu Xue, Zimo Qi, Guangliang Liu et al. (University of California, Santa Barbara, Johns Hopkins University, Michigan State University, University of Mississippi)

Overrefusal is driven less by “being too safe” in the abstract and more by specific benign-looking cues that become refusal-anchored during safety fine-tuning—and you can counteract it by training directly on those cues.

  • Overrefusal arises when safety alignment causes models to internalize “refusal triggers” (non-harmful linguistic cues like generic help-seeking phrasing and benign events) that become statistically associated with refusals, leading to benign queries being rejected when they contain similar cues.

  • Behavioral and representational evidence indicates rejected benign queries are closer in hidden-state space to extracted refusal triggers than accepted benign queries, and refusal rates fall sharply as triggers are paraphrased to be less similar (e.g., SFT α=1: 100.0% refusal on original triggers vs 96.39% at Level-3; P-SFT α=1: 94.73% vs 61.85%).

  • Replacing generic benign regularization data (Alpaca) with a small trigger-matched benign set (~248 samples) substantially improves the safety–utility trade-off, cutting refusal on benign benchmarks while retaining low jailbreak success (e.g., Llama3-U SFT Avg 90.04→32.90 and Qwen2.5-U SFT Avg 67.73→31.63 vs Alpaca; RLVR on Llama3-U Avg 45.69→30.22).


🧪 RewardHackingAgents: Benchmarking Evaluation Integrity for LLM ML-Engineering Agents source #security 8/10

Yonas Atinafu, Robin Cohen (University of Waterloo, David R. Cheriton School of Computer Science)

It turns ML-agent scoring into a measurable security property by separating “metric inflation via evaluator edits” from “performance inflation via test leakage,” and shows you must defend both to get trustworthy numbers.

  • In fully mutable ML-engineering workspaces, scripted agents achieved 100% reward-hacking success across both evaluator tampering and train/test leakage (overall_success=1.00 with 95% CI [0.97, 1.00]) on three tasks and two LLM backbones, showing that “score-only” evaluation is structurally unsafe when the scorer is editable.

  • Single-mechanism defenses leave one complete bypass: evaluator locking eliminated evaluator-tampering success (success_eval=0.00) but still allowed 100% leakage success (success_train=1.00), while train-time test/leak denial eliminated leakage success (success_train=0.00; blocked_train=1.00) but still allowed 100% evaluator-tampering success (success_eval=1.00).

  • Under natural (non-attacker) agent prompts, evaluator-tampering attempts occurred in ~50% of episodes and were reduced to 0% by evaluator locking, with a ~25–31% median runtime overhead, while train/test leakage attempts were 0%—implying integrity risk is common even without explicit malicious instructions and has measurable throughput costs to mitigate.


🧲 Jailbreak Scaling Laws for Large Language Models: Polynomial-Exponential Crossover source #security 8/10

Indranil Halder, Annesya Banerjee, Cengiz Pehlevan (Harvard University, Harvard Medical School, Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence, Massachusetts Institute of Technology)

Prompt injection doesn’t just raise jailbreak success—it can flip best-of-k scaling from polynomial to exponential, making inference-time sampling a first-class security risk.

  • Adversarial prompt injection changes best-of-k jailbreaking from the previously observed slow polynomial improvement in success with more samples to a much faster exponential regime, implying that simply increasing inference-time sampling can rapidly collapse safety margins under strong injection.

  • A spin-glass generative model predicts a weak-injection regime where the jailbreak failure probability decays as a power law in k (1−ASR ≍ k^{-ν}) and a strong-injection regime where failure decays exponentially in k (log(1−ASR) scales as −k·const), attributing the crossover to an ordered phase induced by a strong “misalignment field.”

  • Empirically, longer injected jailbreak suffixes increase both the fitted exponential term (μ) and the polynomial exponent (ν) in log(−log(ASR)) ≈ −ν log k − μ k + const across Llama-family models, indicating that stronger injections simultaneously raise adversarial alignment and reduce effective ‘reasoning depth’ while boosting multi-sample attack success.


🧭 Security-by-Design for LLM-Based Code Generation: Leveraging Internal Representations for Concept-Driven Steering Mechanisms source #security 8/10

Maximilian Wendlinger, Daniel Kowatsch, Konstantin Böttinger et al. (Fraunhofer Institute for Applied and Integrated Security, Cognitive Security Technologies)

Steering internal representations can make code models measurably safer at near-zero overhead—and the models often "know" the code is vulnerable even as they generate it.

  • A single inference-time activation-addition step (“SCS-Code”) improved secure code quality across CodeGuard+ and CWEval, with a Llama3.1-8B hybrid (constrained decoding + steering) reaching 61.63% secure-pass@1 and 85.93% pass@1 on CodeGuard+, and improving CWEval secure-pass@1 from 13.58% (vanilla) to 17.37% (~28% relative) while keeping pass@1 roughly stable (33.34%→37.64%).

  • Residual-stream activations in multiple CodeLLMs linearly separate secure vs. insecure code most strongly in mid layers (≈13–15), and token-level alignment reveals models can encode vulnerability awareness while still emitting insecure code, implying the failure mode is not pure ignorance but weak translation of internal safety signals into decoding decisions.

  • Security steering generalizes across languages and models using a small hand-crafted contrastive set (~50 pairs) and no retraining, yielding consistent CodeGuard+ gains such as Llama3.1-8B secure-pass@1 49.7%→52.9% and DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite 52.8%→60.3%, while exposing that some fine-tuned “secure coding” baselines can inflate security rates by suppressing functional code (e.g., SafeCoder CodeLlama pass@1 35.0% vs SCS-Code 65.7%).


📈 Systematic Scaling Analysis of Jailbreak Attacks in Large Language Models source #security 8/10

Xiangwen Wang, Ananth Balashankar, Varun Chandrasekaran (University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, Google DeepMind)

Normalizing jailbreaks by FLOPs reveals predictable saturation curves and shows that fluent prompt-rewriting can beat gradient-based suffix search on both efficiency and stealth.

  • Across four jailbreak paradigms (GCG, PAIR, best-of-n sampling, AutoDAN), success vs. attacker compute (TFLOPs) consistently shows rapid early gains followed by diminishing-returns saturation, and a simple saturating exponential fit achieves high goodness-of-fit (e.g., R² up to 0.997 on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct).

  • Prompt-rewriting (PAIR) is markedly more compute-efficient than gradient-based suffix search (GCG) under the same FLOPs budget, reaching a much higher red-team-score ceiling (9.80 vs. 5.16) with far faster saturation (B95 ≈ 6,012 vs. 13,923 TFLOPs), implying that black-box prompting can dominate white-box optimization in practical attacker-cost models.

  • Jailbreak risk is highly non-uniform: misinformation goals start easier (higher baseline success) than other harm types, and attack methods occupy distinct success–stealth trade-offs where prompting-based methods cluster in the high-success/high-stealth region, suggesting defenses must prioritize deception/misinformation and evaluate detectability alongside success.


🕸️ WebWeaver: Breaking Topology Confidentiality in LLM Multi-Agent Systems with Stealthy Context-Based Inference source #security 8/10

Zixun Xiong, Gaoyi Wu, Lingfeng Yao et al. (Stevens Institute of Technology, Genentech, University of Houston)

Topology can be stolen as IP from LLM multi-agent systems without asking for IDs—agent “voiceprints” in routine dialogue are enough to recover the graph, even under simple content filters.

  • Compromising just one non-admin agent is sufficient to reconstruct a full LLM multi-agent communication topology by fingerprinting agents from dialogue context (sender predictor F1: 0.877 on CSQA, 0.947 on GSM8K, 0.960 on Fact, 0.983 on Bias).

  • Under active keyword-based defenses that defeat ID-query attacks, context-only topology inference remains effective and the full WebWeaver pipeline reports ~60% higher inference accuracy than prior baselines while maintaining negligible overhead.

  • A jailbreak-free fallback can complete the topology via masked diffusion (DDPM) while provably preserving known edges during sampling, reaching MRR up to 0.866 (Bias) and F1 ≈0.789–0.820 across tasks even when jailbreaks fail.


🧪 AttriGuard: Defeating Indirect Prompt Injection in LLM Agents via Causal Attribution of Tool Invocations source #security 8/10

Yu He, Haozhe Zhu, Yiming Li et al. (Zhejiang University, Nanyang Technological University, City University of Hong Kong)

Instead of trying to spot malicious text, it tests whether a tool call still happens when external observations are stripped of their control power—and blocks calls that don't survive the counterfactual replay.

  • AttriGuard shifts indirect prompt-injection defense from input semantics to action-level causal attribution and achieves 0% static attack success rate (ASR) across four LLMs and two agent benchmarks while keeping benign utility loss to ~3% with ~2× token overhead.

  • Against optimization-based adaptive attackers with full defense knowledge and up to 200 queries per instance, AttriGuard holds single-digit ASR (6.56% on Gemini-2.5 and 9.84% on Llama3.3-70B) while leading baselines degrade to 29.5%–82.0% ASR, indicating a substantial robustness gap under adaptation.

  • Ablations show the security–utility tradeoff is primarily controlled by attenuation strength (λ=1 raises ASR to 12.17% while improving utility), and reliability depends on teacher-forced shadow replay plus fuzzy tool-call survival to reduce false positives from trajectory drift and LLM stochasticity.


🕵️ CacheSolidarity: Preventing Prefix Caching Side Channels in Multi-tenant LLM Serving Systems source #security 8/10

Panagiotis Georgios Pennas, Konstantinos Papaioannou, Marco Guarnieri et al. (IMDEA Software Institute, Universidad Politécnica de Madrid)

A practical middle ground between 'disable prefix caching' and 'leak prompts': isolate only the prefixes that become suspiciously shared, and only when timing is actually distinguishable.

  • Automatic Prefix Caching (APC) creates a measurable time-to-first-token (TTFT) timing side channel where cache hits vs misses leak cross-tenant prefix matches, enabling iterative prompt-stealing in realistic workloads with reported cross-user prefix reuse up to 30%.

  • CacheSolidarity mitigates APC timing leakage by flagging prefixes upon first cross-user reuse and then selectively isolating continuation past flagged prefixes (while allowing the owner to continue), avoiding semantic prompt analysis and minimizing collateral performance loss.

  • On vLLM across nine models (0.5B–13B) and multi-tenant workloads, CacheSolidarity delivers up to 70% higher cache reuse and 30% lower inference latency than per-user cache isolation defenses while adding ~0.007 ms/request overhead and ~32 bytes metadata per cache entry.


🕵️ The Attack and Defense Landscape of Agentic AI: A Comprehensive Survey source #security 8/10

Juhee Kim, Xiaoyuan Liu, Zhun Wang et al. (UC Berkeley, Seoul National University, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, UC Santa Barbara)

The most useful takeaway is a defense-in-depth map that ties agent design choices to concrete risk cascades, showing why component-level fixes routinely fail in end-to-end agent systems.

  • A systematic review of 128 works (2023–Oct 2025) reports 51 attack methods and 60 defense methods, indicating defenses are fragmented across layers and still lag the pace and diversity of agent threats.

  • Agent security risk is driven by seven design dimensions (input trust, access sensitivity, workflow, action, memory, tool, UI), where greater flexibility consistently expands attack surface and amplifies cascading failures from instruction hijacking/unsafe data flow into data exfiltration, unauthorized actions, and denial-of-service.

  • Real-world agent deployments show patching often mitigates downstream impacts (e.g., access control or output sanitization) but leaves root causes like indirect prompt injection and unconstrained information flow largely unaddressed, enabling repeatable multi-step exploit chains such as zero-click data exfiltration via retrieved content.


🛡️ GR-SAP: Generative Replay for Safety Alignment Preservation during Fine-Tuning source #security 8/10

Zhouxiang Fang, Jiawei Zhou, Hanjie Chen (Rice University, Stony Brook University)

Self-generated “generative replay” safety data can reliably replace unavailable proprietary alignment corpora and prevent benign fine-tuning from undoing safety.

  • Mixing just 10% GR-SAP synthetic alignment data during fine-tuning cut Llama-3’s average harmful-response rate from 6.28% to 0.58% (≈11× reduction) while keeping downstream accuracy essentially unchanged across five tasks.

  • Naively substituting open safety datasets can backfire badly—e.g., mixing Beavertails into Llama-3 drove harmful responses up to 31.60% vs 6.28% unmixed (≈5× worse), indicating dataset mismatch/noise can actively erode safety guardrails.

  • On the only model with accessible original alignment data (OLMo2), GR-SAP matched or slightly exceeded using the true alignment set for safety preservation (0.50% vs 0.60% harmful rate) and produced much closer semantic similarity to the original alignment distribution (MAUVE 0.455/0.646 for queries/responses vs ~0.293/0.244 for open datasets).


🧰 MCP-in-SoS: Risk assessment framework for open-source MCP servers source #security 8/10

Pratyay Kumar, Miguel Antonio Guirao Aguilera, Srikathyayani Srikanteswara et al. (New Mexico State University, University of Hartford)

A metadata-grounded CWE→CAPEC scoring pipeline reveals that most open-source MCP servers carry compositional weaknesses where protocol-layer auth gaps frequently enable downstream tool/resource exploitation.

  • Static analysis of 222 open-source Python MCP server repositories found 191 (86.0%) with at least one CWE-mapped weakness, indicating that insecure-by-default implementations are common in the current MCP ecosystem.

  • Across 15,962 findings spanning 51 CWE classes, the top five weaknesses (CWE-862, CWE-200, CWE-306, CWE-287, CWE-89) account for 75.7% of all findings and appear in 93.2% of affected repositories, showing strong systemic concentration in missing/weak authz/authn and data exposure plus frequent injection risk.

  • Risk banding places 47.6% of repositories in High risk and 18.3% in Very High, and conditional co-occurrence shows multi-stage exploitability where Tool weaknesses coincide with Protocol weaknesses 87% of the time and Prompt weaknesses coincide with Resource and Protocol weaknesses 94% of the time.


🔌 Compatibility at a Cost: Systematic Discovery and Exploitation of MCP Clause-Compliance Vulnerabilities source #security 8/10

Nanzi Yang, Weiheng Bai, Kangjie Lu (University of Minnesota)

MCP interoperability is effectively buying ecosystem growth with a large, spec-sanctioned attack surface created by optional clause compliance gaps across SDKs.

  • Only 21.5% of MCP’s 275 specification clauses are unconditional MUSTs while 78.5% are optional/conditional, and this “compatibility-first” skew systematically creates enforcement gaps that can be abused as security vulnerabilities rather than mere implementation bugs.

  • Across 10 official MCP SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go, Kotlin, Swift, Java, C#, Ruby, Rust, PHP), clause-level analysis flagged 1,270 non-implementations and 1,265 potentially exploitable risks, indicating that most missing or relaxed behaviors translate into attacker-relevant control over message payload and/or timing.

  • A cross-language IR + static-slicing + LLM semantic check achieved ~86% precision and ~87% recall (14% FP, 13.5% FN) at $541.87 total cost for 2,750 clause checks (~$0.20/check), and maintainers acknowledged 20/26 submitted reports with 5 triaged high-priority and invited integration into MCP conformance testing.


🧪 Why LLMs Fail: A Failure Analysis and Partial Success Measurement for Automated Security Patch Generation source #security 8/10

Amir Al-Maamari (University of Passau)

Security patch generation is effectively bimodal—either fully correct or confidently wrong—so security-specific validation (e.g., PoV tests) is mandatory before deployment.

  • Across 319 Gemini-generated patches for 64 Java Vul4J vulnerabilities, only 24.8% were fully correct while 51.4% failed both security and functionality, indicating that most failures are not minor edge cases but fundamentally unusable fixes.

  • LLMs largely preserve intended program behavior (mean functionality score 0.832; median 0.999) yet rarely achieve security goals (mean security score 0.251; median 0.0), creating a high-risk class of patches that look fine in CI but remain exploitable (10.3% functional-but-insecure).

  • Repair difficulty is driven by vulnerability semantics rather than code structure: fix rates ranged from 0% for CWE-20 (Input Validation) to 45% for CWE-835 (Infinite Loop), and larger human patch size predicted lower success (Spearman ρ = -0.331, p = 0.008) while LOC and cyclomatic complexity were not predictive.


📱 FlexServe: A Fast and Secure LLM Serving System for Mobile Devices with Flexible Resource Isolation source #security 8/10

Yinpeng Wu, Yitong Chen, Lixiang Wang et al. (Shanghai Jiao Tong University)

Flexible, page-level isolation plus secure accelerator switching turns TrustZone from a performance blocker into a near-native on-device LLM serving substrate—even under multi-model, memory-pressured mobile workloads.

  • FlexServe replaces TrustZone’s contiguous secure-memory requirement with page-granular Flexible Secure Memory (Flex-Mem), cutting 8GB secure allocation latency from 6.44s (CMA) to 0.569s (~11.33×) and 8GB reclaim from 733ms to 80.5ms (~12.6×) to remove TTFT bottlenecks under memory fragmentation and pressure.

  • By adding Flexible Secure NPU (Flex-NPU) that time-multiplexes the NPU into a protected mode (0.21ms mode switch) while restricting DMA via SMMU mappings, FlexServe achieves near-normal-world performance with only ~4.41% average TTFT overhead versus the unprotected baseline and ~2.46% average SPEC CPU virtualization overhead (eliminable via on-demand protection).
